title: Lesson Quiz: Types of Income and Earnings
description: Assessment of the concept that Wages, salaries, hourly vs. salary, and income sources.

choices.text: Income earned for each hour worked
choices.feedback: Correct. Hourly income depends on the number of hours worked.

choices.text: A fixed yearly amount no matter how many hours are worked
A tax collected from each paycheck
choices.feedback: Hourly income is a type of earnings, not a tax.

choices.text: Money spent after a paycheck is received
choices.feedback: Hourly income refers to earnings, not spending.

questions.prompt: A salary is usually a fixed amount paid over a time period.

choices.feedback: Salary usually refers to a fixed amount paid over a set period.
Correct. Salary income is usually more fixed than hourly pay.

questions.prompt: A worker earns $14 per hour for 15 hours. What are the total earnings?

choices.feedback: This total is too low. Multiply the hourly rate by the full 15 hours.
$224 would be too high for 15 hours at $14 per hour.

questions.prompt: Why can a job with a lower hourly wage sometimes lead to higher weekly income?

choices.text: Because hourly wage is the only factor that matters
choices.feedback: Total hours also affect weekly income.

choices.text: Because it may offer more working hours, which raises total earnings
choices.feedback: This is a strong reason, but the correct answer in this set more clearly connects total hours to weekly income.

choices.text: Because total weekly income depends on both pay rate and hours worked
choices.feedback: This idea is related, but it does not directly explain how more hours can still produce higher weekly earnings.

choices.text: Because more hours can produce a higher total even when the hourly rate is lower
choices.feedback: Correct. Weekly income depends on both the rate and the number of hours worked.

questions.prompt: Comparing income options should include total earnings, not just the hourly rate.

choices.feedback: Correct. Total earnings can change when hours differ, even if hourly rates are not the same.
Strong income comparisons should include total earnings, not just the hourly rate.
